topic: Error Handling - miette for Rich Diagnostics

turns.content: Hey, I'm learning Rust and error handling. I've done some reading about std::error::Error but I'm curious how to make user-facing errors more informative — like Rust compiler error messages with line numbers.

That's a great goal! You want rich diagnostics that help users understand where things went wrong. miette is an excellent crate for this. It extends std::error::Error to provide source spans and colorful, user-friendly error messages — kind of like how the Rust compiler shows you exactly which line caused a compile error.
Doesn't that sound similar to what I've seen in Haskell's exception handling? But then again, Rust is so explicit about error types; does miette fit with idiomatic Rust?

Absolutely! In fact, miette integrates well because it derives from std::error::Error. You just need to annotate your errors with source spans (via `miette::Diagnostic`) and implement the relevant traits manually or derive them. It's very idiomatic Rust — you get all the benefits of explicit error handling without compromising on developer experience.
Okay, so I'd annotate my errors with source spans when they happen, then wrap everything in a Result and pass it to miette? And how do I integrate this into CLI tools?

Exactly! When you have an error of your type (say `MyError`), you just call `.wrap_err()` or `.context()` before passing it on. For example, if you use clap for CLI args and want to display errors in a user-friendly way when something goes wrong, miette’s pretty printers will handle that. It's like giving your CLI app colorful compiler-like diagnostics.
I get it, so I annotate my error types with source spans, wrap them with miette, and then display them in a CLI tool? And if I use serde_json or similar crates, will they work fine?

Good question! Since `miette` just enhances the error message but doesn't interfere with serialization, you can still serialize your errors — maybe by serializing the underlying error type. Just be careful that the spans don’t get lost when converting to JSON. You might want to avoid including source spans in serialized output or strip them out before serialization.
Ah, so I shouldn't include source spans in JSON? Otherwise users won't know where errors occurred?

Right! You can leave those for the CLI or terminal display but strip them out before serializing to avoid leaking internal details. That's a good practice — keep error output friendly for humans, but safe and concise in logs or APIs.
